ac:rich-text-body.p: Govern the LiveLabs workshop portfolio as a repeatable portfolio-management process, not as a one-time retirement exercise.
This page is the source of truth for how LiveLabs identifies Top Performers, Top Retire Candidates, and Workshops To Retire, using demand, staleness, ownership, and replacement evidence.
This strategy applies to workshops only.
This governance model balances two portfolio needs at the same time:

ul.li: protect and invest in strong workshops
identify aging, low-value, or superseded workshops for refresh or retirement

ac:rich-text-body.p: Non-goals: lifetime-view ranking, repo-level activity as a workshop proxy, and retirement based on a single weak signal.

ul.li: Top Performers for protection and investment
Top Retire Candidates for broad review prioritization
Workshops To Retire for strongest retirement actions
Demand: recent_views_90d and recent_views_12m
Age: age_months since publish
Staleness: months_since_update from meaningful workshop updates
Ownership: owner_present
Replacement: replacement_exists and replacement_similarity_score
Keep when value is healthy and content is current enough
Refresh when value is high but staleness is rising
Retire Candidate when evidence supports retirement review
Retired only after approval and execution

ol.li: Start with Governance Framework to understand the outputs, decisions, and operating flow.
Read Goals & Success Criteria and Core Principles for the governing intent.
Read Lifecycle Decision Criteria and Scoring Model to understand how workshop states are assigned and prioritized.
Use Demand Thresholds, Workshop Staleness, and Replacement Criteria as the evidence basis for governance.
Use Governance Calculation Summary as the compact formula reference.

p: Workshop governance is the portfolio process used to decide where LiveLabs should invest, where it should refresh, and where it should retire.
The page is organized to answer three governance questions in order: what should be protected, what should be reviewed, and what is strong enough for retirement action.

td.p: Which workshops create the most current portfolio value?
best_performer_score = 0.60 x recent-demand percentile + 0.40 x yearly-demand percentile.
Protect, promote, and refresh high-value workshops before considering retirement.
Which workshops deserve governance review first?
retire_score blends low demand, staleness, replacement evidence, and ownership risk.
Prioritize review conversations, cleanup planning, and replacement validation.
Which workshops have the strongest evidence for retirement action now?
Strict subset of retire candidates: mature age, strong staleness, low demand, and confirmed replacement support.
Move to approval, website removal, and repository archive steps.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Detail: Each output can be published as a ranked top depending on the governance audience.

td.p: Workshop remains in the active portfolio without near-term intervention.
Protect valuable or sufficiently current content.
Workshop remains valuable but update pressure is rising.
Invest in content that still serves users but is drifting.
Workshop enters structured governance review for possible retirement.
Focus review attention where value is weak or redundancy is rising.
Workshop is removed from active LiveLabs delivery.
Complete portfolio cleanup only after approval and execution.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Important: Top Performers is a ranking output, not a lifecycle state.

ol.li: Measure the portfolio using current demand, workshop age, meaningful update staleness, owner coverage, and replacement evidence.
Rank Top Performers to identify workshops that should be protected and invested in.
Rank Top Retire Candidates to identify workshops that deserve review attention first.
Apply strict retirement gates to isolate the smaller set of Workshops To Retire.
Move through owner review, approval, and execution before any workshop is actually retired.

ul.li: Use recent_views_90d and recent_views_12m, not lifetime views.
Normalize retirement-demand pressure within category using views_12m_percentile.
Use meaningful workshop-level update evidence, not broad repo maintenance activity.
Treat replacement_exists = true as the only replacement state that can support strict retirement.
Use scoring to prioritize review, but use lifecycle gates and approval to make final retirement decisions.
Protect and invest in the highest-value workshops in the portfolio
Refresh workshops that still matter but are becoming stale
Retire workshops that are low-value, aging, and credibly superseded
Make portfolio decisions explainable, repeatable, and auditable
Top-performer outputs are published and used to guide protection and refresh investment
High-demand stale workshops move into Refresh with owners and target dates
Retire-candidate and to-retire outputs are reviewed on schedule with documented rationale
Yearly portfolio review completed on schedule
Retired workshops are removed from LiveLabs and GitHub archived with documented replacement or obsolescence rationale
Portfolio lifecycle, not one-off cleanup: decisions repeat on a yearly portfolio review cycle today and should remain consistent as the process matures.
Automation is a future improvement: workshop update checks may be automated later, but the current scope is the yearly portfolio governance review.
Do not retire using lifetime views: use recent_views_90d and recent_views_12m so current relevance drives decisions.
Normalize demand within category: use percentiles within category/repo/track so niche workshops are not penalized against broad intro workshops.
Repo activity is not a workshop signal: large repos contain many workshop generations; evaluate at the workshop level when possible.

Last updated must be meaningful: use the latest meaningful workshop-path content update, not broad maintenance-only touches such as Adobe Analytics cookie removals, global URL-fix sweeps, or template-only cleanup.
Superseded does not mean unimportant: older workshops often have similar replacements; replace/redirect rather than leaving duplicates.
Scope clarity: this policy is for workshops. Event content is not scored here.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Refresh the demand, staleness, ownership, age, and replacement signals used by governance.
Rank the strongest workshops to guide protection, featuring, and refresh investment.
Rank Top Retire Candidates, then apply strict retirement gates to isolate Workshops To Retire.
Owners either commit to refresh with dates, validate retirement, or challenge weak evidence.
Refresh, keep, or retire workshops and document the final rationale for portfolio traceability.

p: This section explains how the refresh-side and retire-side signals translate into lifecycle outcomes.
The visible summary table is the normal reading path. The detailed matrix and the gates are collapsed because they are reference logic.

td.p: Workshop remains active without near-term intervention.
Normal maintenance and continued portfolio use.
Workshop still creates value, but update pressure is high enough to justify work.
Assign or confirm owner, define scope, and set a due date.
Workshop enters structured review for possible retirement.
Validate replacement or obsolescence, review with owner, and decide whether to retire or refresh.
Workshop is removed from active LiveLabs delivery.
Remove from website, archive repository, and record the rationale.

ac:rich-text-body.p: This summary table groups the decision logic into the outcome families that matter most during portfolio review.

td.p: Do not push valuable workshops into retire-side logic too early.
High-value stale content should be updated rather than retired.
Low demand alone is not enough for retire-side action.
Low demand + Aging + No owner + replacement evidence
Early candidate entry is allowed when weak value combines with weak ownership and credible replacement evidence.
Replacement strength determines whether the workshop should be fixed first or moved into retirement review.
Low demand + Stale or Critical + replacement evidence present
This is the main entry path for retire-side filtering.
Retire Candidate + age_months >= 30 + months_since_update >= 24 + views_12m_percentile <= 10 + recent_views_90d < 25 + replacement_exists = true
Workshops To Retire / Retired after approval
This is the strict retirement subset, not the default retire-candidate outcome.

ac:rich-text-body.p: This matrix shows all combinations of the triage criteria used in the decision framework. It drives the initial lifecycle recommendation before age, approval, and strict-retirement gates are applied.

td.p: Keep active; watch for persistent weakness.
Keep active; watch for persistent weakness.
Keep active; watch for persistent weakness.
Keep active, watchlist it, and assign owner.
Keep active, watchlist it, and assign owner.
Keep active, watchlist it, and assign owner.
Watchlist; review again if staleness worsens.
Watchlist; review again if staleness worsens.
Watchlist; review again if staleness worsens.
Weak ownership plus replacement evidence can move it into review.
Validate replacement and review for retirement.
Watchlist and assign owner; not strong enough to retire yet.
Refresh if value may still be recoverable; no replacement proof yet.
Weak ownership plus replacement evidence supports retire review.
Owner gap and staleness justify retire-side review even without replacement proof.
Retire-side review is justified; replacement or obsolescence still must be validated.
Retire-side review is justified; replacement or obsolescence still must be validated.

ac:rich-text-body.p: These gates sit on top of the matrix and explain when a matrix outcome is allowed to move into stricter retire-side subsets.

ul.li: Category normalization gate: final low-demand decisions must use views_12m_percentile within category, not global raw views.
Healthy-demand override: workshops above the category median should default away from retire-side action and toward Keep or Refresh.
Staleness gate: Fresh and most Aging workshops normally do not enter the retire-side path unless business obsolescence is documented.
Age gate: age_months < 24 normally blocks retire-candidate status. age_months 24-29 can support Retire Candidate but not the strict to-retire subset.
Strict retirement subset gate: require age_months >= 30, months_since_update >= 24, recent_views_90d < 25, views_12m_percentile <= 10, and replacement_exists = true.

Replacement gate: replacement_exists = true is the only replacement state that can support the strict to-retire subset. review requires validation. false requires documented obsolescence before retirement can complete.
Owner gate: missing owner increases retire-side pressure, but high-demand stale workshops should still default to Refresh with owner assignment.
Approval gate: no workshop moves to Retired automatically; retirement requires owner review and governance approval.
Auto-flag rules: auto-flag anything with months_since_update >= 18, views_12m_percentile <= 10, or no owner plus stale content.

p: This section defines the raw fields and derived metrics used by the decision framework and the scoring model.
It is a reference section, so the detailed field table is collapsed by default.

td.p: Best signal for current demand shifts.
Used for yearly demand comparisons and category normalization.
Percentile rank of recent_views_12m inside the workshop's category
Primary low-demand signal for retire-side decisions.
Months since the workshop was published
Used as a maturity gate, not as a stand-alone retirement signal.
Months since the last meaningful workshop content update
Prefer workshop-level evidence; repo-level evidence is lower confidence.
Usually repo, track, or product grouping.
Whether a named DRI exists for the workshop
Composite similarity score to the strongest replacement candidate
Replacement state derived from similarity scoring: true / review / false
Only true supports the strict to-retire subset.
Blended score for ranking top-performing workshops
Used to rank review priority, not to retire automatically.

p: This section explains how governance prioritizes review work after lifecycle outcomes are assigned.
Scoring helps rank what should be reviewed first. It does not replace the decision framework, the gates, or approval.

ac:rich-text-body.p: retire_score (0-100) = 40 x low_demand_score + 30 x staleness_score + 20 x superseded_score + 10 x no_owner_score

td.p: How weak the workshop is inside its category.
How old the last meaningful update is.
Whether the workshop has no owner.
1.0 if replacement_exists = true; otherwise 0.0. review and false do not receive superseded score until replacement is confirmed or obsolescence is documented.
Based on category percentile, not global raw views.
Use months since last meaningful content update.
Only use when a valid replacement exists.
No named owner increases retirement risk.

p.strong: Example outcome: prioritized retire candidate for review

td.p: Prioritized retire candidate - strong review signal, but still subject to gates and approval.
Retire candidate review queue - review is justified, but evidence still needs validation.
Keep or Refresh path - use demand and staleness together to decide whether the workshop stays active or moves into refresh work.

ul.li: Always calculate views_12m_percentile within category; do not compare all workshops globally.
The score is used to prioritize review, not to automatically retire a workshop.
Final decisions must still respect replacement availability, owner review, and governance approval.

td.p: 0.60 x percentile_rank(recent_views_90d_global) + 0.40 x percentile_rank(recent_views_12m_global), scaled to 0-100
Ranks the strongest workshops by balancing current and stable demand.
40 x low_demand_score + 30 x staleness_score + 20 x superseded_score + 10 x no_owner_score
Prioritizes retire-side review by weighting low demand and staleness most heavily.
Preserves category-relative demand as the primary retire-side signal.
Separates normal maintenance pressure from true retire-side staleness.
Keeps strict retirement anchored to strong replacement confidence.
Adds retire-side pressure when no clear maintainer exists.

p: These sections document the portfolio evidence used to justify demand, staleness, and replacement thresholds.
They support governance decisions, but they do not replace the decision framework or the scoring model above.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Table is created based on data extracted on 18.03.2026 from LiveLabs Dashboard.
Scope is to guide selection criteria and thresholds.

ul.li: Mean: arithmetic average of all values. It is sensitive to very high or very low values, so in skewed datasets it can be pulled away from the typical workshop.
Median: middle value when all workshops are ordered from lowest to highest. It is more robust than the mean in skewed data and is often a better indicator of a “typical” workshop.
Std Dev (Standard Deviation): shows how widely values are spread around the mean. A higher value means workshop views vary more across the portfolio.
Q1: first quartile, meaning 25% of workshops are at or below this value. It can be used as a sanity-check low-demand anchor.
Q3: third quartile, meaning 75% of workshops are at or below this value. It helps identify the upper end of the normal demand range.

Upper Bound: calculated as Q3 + 1.5 x IQR. Values above this level are considered unusually high-traffic outliers and may help identify stale workshops that should be prioritized for Refresh rather than retirement.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Important: these are portfolio interpretation bands, not the primary retirement-governance rule. Final retirement decisions should still use views_12m_percentile within category so niche workshop categories are not penalized by global raw-volume differences.
This section defines Views Thresholds based on the Portfolio Distribution Statistics.

td.p:
Bottom 10% in category
Absolute anchor: around < 141
Strong retirement review signal when combined with staleness, missing owner, or replacement availability.
25 views in 90d is already aligned to the current Retired rule. 141 views in 12m is close to the yearly Q1 anchor and helps flag very weak annual demand.
Use as yearly cleanup watchlist; usually not enough alone to retire, but supports Retire Candidate when the workshop is stale and superseded.
31 is the Q1 anchor for the 90-day distribution. 141 is the Q1 anchor for the yearly distribution.
Default middle band. Do not retire based on views alone; evaluate staleness and ownership before action.
This band spans from Q1 to median, which is a practical representation of normal portfolio demand.
Usually Keep. If stale, prefer Refresh over retirement.
This range spans from median to Q3, meaning demand is at or above a typical workshop level.
High-value workshops. If stale, these should be prioritized for Refresh, not retirement.
This band starts at Q3 and extends to the IQR Upper Bound, representing strong but not extreme demand.
Do not retire without explicit exception review. These are strong candidates for maintenance or refresh investment.
These values are above the IQR Upper Bound, which identifies unusually high-traffic outliers in the portfolio.

ac:rich-text-body.p: This section defines workshop staleness thresholds.

Implementation note: months_since_update should be calculated from the latest meaningful workshop-path content update. Ignore maintenance-only commits such as Adobe Analytics cookie removals, global URL-fix sweeps, and template-only cleanup when an older content-level update exists.

Source basis: the statistical justification below uses the current WMS portfolio joined to meaningful workshop update evidence. Threshold selection is anchored on 1,033 workshops with high-confidence meaningful update dates.
Coverage note: workshops without usable meaningful-update evidence should not move into staleness-driven retirement action until the update signal is validated manually.
This table plays the same role for staleness that the portfolio distribution table plays for demand: it provides the statistical baseline used to define the governance bands.
the portfolio is not centered around recently updated workshops.
The median meaningful update age is 18.73 months, which means staleness pressure is common across the portfolio.
At the same time, workshops above roughly 30 months are statistically unusual and should be treated as extreme cases.

td.p: Workshop is reasonably current for yearly review purposes.
Aligned with the current Keep rule and closely matches the portfolio Q1 anchor of 11.01 months.
Workshop may still be usable, but content drift risk is increasing.
Creates a practical buffer between the fresh band and the portfolio median, without turning common drift into automatic retirement pressure.
High chance that instructions, screenshots, or dependencies are out of date.
Refresh if demand is healthy; Retire Candidate if demand is low and replacement exists.
Anchored around the portfolio median / Q3 of 18.73 months and aligned with the current governance rule that workshops at 18 months or more should be flagged for review.
Strong indication of portfolio risk, even before entering the statistical outlier range.
Retire Candidate or Retired, unless there is strong demand and a committed refresh path.
Aligned with the highest staleness score band in the scoring model and the two-year governance anchor of 24 months for strong retirement pressure.
Statistically unusual staleness and strong evidence that the workshop has fallen far outside normal portfolio maintenance.
Anchored to the portfolio IQR upper bound of roughly 30.31 months, which is a cleaner justification for extreme staleness than using an arbitrary cutoff.

ac:rich-text-body.p: This section defines how replacement evidence is calculated and how it is used in portfolio governance.
Formula: replacement_similarity_score = 0.35 x title_similarity + 0.35 x content_similarity + 0.15 x category_similarity + 0.15 x recency_similarity

td.p: Weighted blend of sequence similarity and token overlap between normalized workshop titles.
Captures close naming or rebranding patterns.
Overlap across workshop metadata/content including description, summary, content text, URLs, category, and repo indicators.
Captures overlap in actual workshop subject matter and assets.
100 when source and candidate are in the same category, else 0.
Prevents cross-category false positives from dominating the score.
Higher score when the potential replacement is newer and the publish-date gap is small.
Biases toward modern successors rather than parallel or legacy workshops.
Eligible for strict retirement gating when other criteria also pass.
Use for replacement validation, not as final retirement proof by itself.
Do not use as retirement proof without a separate documented obsolescence rationale.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Governance rule: only replacement_exists = true should support strict retirement decisions. review requires human confirmation, and false requires either stronger evidence or an explicit obsolescence rationale.
